titleStmt.title: Letter from Francis Caldwell, Head Constable, Liverpool, to the Officer Commanding the Irish National Volunteers, Dublin, 31 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Caldwell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter from Francis Caldwell to the Officer commanding the Irish National Volunteers encloses a police report referring to and describing an alleged deserter, Joseph Egan, under the Military Service Act. Also enclosed is the response from Patrick J Reddy of the Irish National Volunteers, declaring no knowledge of the individual in question. Francis Caldwell (c.1860-1934) was the Head Constable of Liverpool at the time of writing this letter. He died at 74 as a decorated officer. The Military Service Act introduced conscription on 27 January 1916. It made men aged between 19 and 41, who had lived in Britain since August 1915 and had no dependent children enlist for general service with the British Armed Forces. The men now came under the controls specified in the Army Act.

correspAction.persName: Officer Commanding the Irish National Volunteers

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Telephone No. 8300 Central.Telegrams: 'Devoir, Liverpool.' Please address your letter
THE HEAD CONSTABLE,
and in your reply quote No. Your reference No. Central Police Office, LIVERPOOL 31 March 1916. I beg to send the attached copy of Police Report for your information. Yours faithfully, FRANCIS CALDWELL,
Head Constable. The Officer Commanding.
Irish National Volunteers.
Dublin
'H'. General Enquiry Office 29 3 16. P.C. 151 'H' Constantine. Inspector on duty. Mar 10 Alleged deserter under the Military
Service Act.
I beg to report that at 6-25.pm.today Mr
Thomas Dickson, Special Constable. (E. Division) called
at this Office and stated that a Man, named Joseph E-
gan,residing in Almond Street,No not known, had absconded to Ireland after having been served with his pap- ers to attend for Military duties .Complt states that
accused is believed to be in Dublin at a Camp of the
Irish National Volunteers, he is described as follows,
29 to 33yrs,5ft 7 1/2',dark hair and heavy moustache;
usually dresses very respectably, speaks with a broad
Irish accent and up to 18th inst he was employed at
Messrs Stonier & Co.78 Lord Street,when he left re-
marking to a friend that things were getting too hot.
Constantine P.C.151'H'. Submitted Ch. Sup of H. C. O.
ÃGLÃIGH TÃORAMHLA NA H-ÃIREANN, â IRISH NATIONAL VOLUNTEERS. Left-hand side of page TELEPHONE 4598 DUBLIN, TELEGRAMS: 'VOLUNTEERS. Ref. No. 155 COMMUNICATIONS TO BE ADDRESSED TO THE INSPECTOR GENERAL Inspector General's Office, 44, Parnell Square, Dublin. Date, 3rd. April 1916. The Head Constable,
Central Police Office
Liverpool .
Sir, The Inspector General is in receipt of your memorandum of the
31st March re Joseph Egan and begs to inform you that the National
Volunteers have at present no Camp in the City or County of Dublin. We do not know anything of the man referred to. I have the honour to be,
Sir,
Your obedient servant, Patrick J.Reddy
Secretary to the Inspector General.
